Made with MyST

Atomic spectra

What you will learn

  • The energies of atoms, molecules, and light are quantized, and they can only take on specific, discrete values rather than a continuous spectrum.

  • This quantization is a cornerstone of physical reality, confirmed by countless experiments and rigorously explained by quantum mechanics.

  • Pivotal historical discoveries, such as blackbody radiation, the photoelectric effect, and the double-slit experiment, laid the foundation for quantum theory.

  • Everyday macroscopic phenomena, including the red glow of heated metals, the heat capacity of solids at low temperatures, and the characteristic colors of materials, all originate from quantum effects.

Spectroscopy of Atoms¶

  • Spectroscopy is the study of the interaction between matter and electromagnetic radiation.

  • By analyzing the emitted or absorbed light, spectroscopy reveals information about the structure and composition of atoms and molecules.

  • When heated or subjected to electrical discharge, atoms emit radiation at characteristic frequencies. The resulting spectrum is unique for each element, serving as a kind of atomic fingerprint.

Spectral lines and Rydberg’s formula¶

  • The existence of discrete spectral lines is impossible to describe with classical mechanics. In 1885, Johann Balmer demonstrated that a subset of the hydrogen atom spectrum (the Balmer series) could be described by the equation

ν~=8.2202×1014(1−4n2)\tilde{\nu} = 8.2202\times10^{14}\left(1-\frac{4}{n^2}\right)ν~=8.2202×1014(1−n24​)
(1)

where n=3,4,5,...n=3,4,5,...n=3,4,5,.... Later, Johannes Rydberg generalized this formula to account for the entire hydrogen atom spectrum yielding the Rydberg formula

Rydberg formula
ν~=RH(1n12−1n22)\tilde{\nu} = R_H\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)ν~=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)
(2)

where

  • RH=1.097×107 m−1R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \ \text{m}^{-1}RH​=1.097×107 m−1 is the Rydberg constant.

  • n1=1,2,3,...n_1 = 1,2,3,...n1​=1,2,3,..., and n2=n1+1,n1+2,...n_2 = n_1+1,n_1+2,...n2​=n1​+1,n1​+2,....

  • While these equations fit the hydrogen atom spectrum nicely, they do not prescribe any physics to the system. They do not present a model of the hydrogen atom but rather a heuristic equation that fits the data. Nonetheless, scientists were perplexed by the presence of the integers n1n_1n1​ and n2n_2n2​.

Bohr’s Model of the Hydrogen Atom¶

  • In 1913, Niels Bohr proposed a model of the hydrogen atom that successfully explained its discrete emission spectrum.

  • The atom was pictured as an electron moving in circular orbits around a central proton. Because the proton is far more massive than the electron, it was treated as fixed in space.

  • To prevent the electron from spiraling into the nucleus, Bohr introduced a new quantization rule: the electron’s orbital motion must accommodate an integer number of standing wave modes, n=1,2,3,…n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsn=1,2,3,…

  • This postulate leads directly to an expression for the allowed energy levels of hydrogen, each labeled by a principal quantum number nnn.

Quantizing the States of the Electron in the Hydrogen Atom¶

  • Imposing this condition gives the relation

2πr=nλe,n=1,2,3,…2\pi r = n \lambda_e, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots2πr=nλe​,n=1,2,3,…
(3)

  • Here, λe\lambda_eλe​ is the de Broglie wavelength of the electron:

λe=hmev.\lambda_e = \frac{h}{m_e v}.λe​=me​vh​.
(4)

  • Substituting this expression for λe\lambda_eλe​ into the quantization condition yields

mevr=nh2π=nℏ.m_e v r = \frac{n h}{2\pi} = n \hbar.me​vr=2πnh​=nℏ.
(5)

  • We introduce the shorthand ℏ=h2π\hbar = \tfrac{h}{2\pi}ℏ=2πh​ because it appears frequently in quantum mechanics. The left-hand side, mevrm_e v rme​vr, represents the angular momentum of the electron.

  • Thus, Bohr’s model predicts that the electron’s angular momentum is quantized in integer multiples of ℏ\hbarℏ.

Force Balance¶

After introducing his quantization rule, Bohr turned back to classical mechanics to determine the allowed electron energies. He assumed that, in a stationary orbit, the electrostatic attraction between the proton and electron is exactly balanced by the centrifugal force of the orbiting electron.

Electrostatic force

fel=e24πε0r2,f_{\text{el}} = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2},fel​=4πε0​r2e2​,
(6)

where eee is the elementary charge and the factor 4πε04\pi \varepsilon_04πε0​ ensures SI units.

Centrifugal force

fcf=mev2r,f_{\text{cf}} = \frac{m_e v^2}{r},fcf​=rme​v2​,
(7)

where mem_eme​ is the electron mass and vvv its orbital velocity.

Equating these two forces gives

e24πε0r2=mev2r.\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{m_e v^2}{r}.4πε0​r2e2​=rme​v2​.
(8)

The force-balance equation together with the quantized angular momentum condition restricts the allowed radii rrr of electron orbits. Solving step by step:

  1. From angular momentum quantization:

    mevr=nℏ⇒v=nℏmer.m_e v r = n\hbar \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{n\hbar}{m_e r}.me​vr=nℏ⇒v=me​rnℏ​.
    (9)

  2. Substituting into the force-balance equation:

    e24πε0r2=mer(nℏmer)2.\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{m_e}{r} \left( \frac{n\hbar}{m_e r} \right)^2.4πε0​r2e2​=rme​​(me​rnℏ​)2.
    (10)

  3. Simplifying:

    e24πε0=(nℏ)2mer.\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = \frac{(n\hbar)^2}{m_e r}.4πε0​e2​=me​r(nℏ)2​.
    (11)

  4. Solving for rrr:

    r=4πε0(nℏ)2mee2=n2a0,n=1,2,3,…r = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon_0 (n\hbar)^2}{m_e e^2} = n^2 a_0, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsr=me​e24πε0​(nℏ)2​=n2a0​,n=1,2,3,…
    (12)

  • where the constant a0a_0a0​ is the Bohr radius, corresponding to the size of the ground-state orbit.

  • We see clearly that the radius of an orbit grows with increasing quantum number n=1,2,3n=1,2,3n=1,2,3.

Bohr radius
a0=4πε0ℏ2mee2a_0 = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon_0 \hbar^2}{m_e e^2}a0​=me​e24πε0​ℏ2​
(13)
a0≈0.529 A˚a_0 \approx 0.529 \,\text{Å}a0​≈0.529A˚
(14)

  • We will encounter the Bohr radius many times. It sets the fundamental length scale for atomic physics!

Energy of the Hydrogen Atom¶

The total energy of the electron–proton system is the sum of the electron’s kinetic energy and the Coulomb potential energy:

E(r)=12mev2−e24πε0r.E(r) = \tfrac{1}{2} m_e v^2 - \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}.E(r)=21​me​v2−4πε0​re2​.
(15)

Using the force-balance relation

mev2=e24πε0r,m_e v^2 = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r},me​v2=4πε0​re2​,
(16)

we substitute into the energy expression:

E(r)=12e24πε0r−e24πε0r=−12e24πε0r.\begin{align} E(r) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r} - \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r} \\ &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}. \end{align}E(r)​=21​4πε0​re2​−4πε0​re2​=−21​4πε0​re2​.​​
(17)

Next, inserting the quantized orbital radius

r=4πε0(nℏ)2mee2,r = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon_0 (n\hbar)^2}{m_e e^2},r=me​e24πε0​(nℏ)2​,
(18)

gives the Bohr energy levels:

En=−mee48ε02h2⋅1n2,n=1,2,3,…E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsEn​=−8ε02​h2me​e4​⋅n21​,n=1,2,3,…
(19)
Bohr Energy formula
En=−13.61n2   [eV]E_n = -13.6\frac{ 1}{n^2} \,\,\,[eV]En​=−13.6n21​[eV]
(20)

  • This is the most useful form for problem solving. You can quickly compute the energy of any hydrogenic level just by inserting the principal quantum number nnn.

  • Ionization energy corresponds to taking the electron from n=1n=1n=1 to n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞, requiring exactly 13.6 eV.

  • You can compute the energy (or frequency) of the photon for a jump from state nnn to mmm by taking the difference between energy levels: ΔEn→m=−19.6(1/m2−1/n2)=hν\Delta E_{n\rightarrow m} = -19.6 (1/m^2-1/n^2) = h\nuΔEn→m​=−19.6(1/m2−1/n2)=hν

Spectral lines and the Rydberg constant¶

The energy difference between two levels n1n_1n1​ and n2n_2n2​ is

ΔE=mee48ε02h2(1n12−1n22).\Delta E = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 h^2} \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right).ΔE=8ε02​h2me​e4​(n12​1​−n22​1​).
(21)

Relating this to photon energy E=hνE = h\nuE=hν and the wavenumber ν~=ν/c\tilde{\nu} = \nu/cν~=ν/c gives

ν~=mee48ε02ch3(1n12−1n22)=RH(1n12−1n22),\tilde{\nu} = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 c h^3} \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) = R_H \left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right),ν~=8ε02​ch3me​e4​(n12​1​−n22​1​)=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​),
(22)

where RHR_HRH​ is the Rydberg constant, which we now know expressed in fundamental constants rather than obtained as the result of an experimental fit!

RH=mee48ε02ch3R_H = \frac{m_e e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0^2 c h^3}RH​=8ε02​ch3me​e4​
(23)
A note about wavenumbers and cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1 units

  • Wavenumbers ν~\tilde{\nu}ν~ in cm⁻¹ are standard in spectroscopy.

  • To convert to wavelength: λ=1/ν~\lambda = 1 / \tilde{\nu}λ=1/ν~ (with ν~\tilde{\nu}ν~ in cm⁻¹, λ\lambdaλ will come out in cm).

ν~ (cm−1)=RH(1n12−1n22),n2>n1,\tilde{\nu}\ \text{(cm}^{-1}\text{)} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right), \quad n_2 > n_1,ν~ (cm−1)=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​),n2​>n1​,
(24)

  • RH=1.097×105 cm−1R_H = 1.097 \times 10^5 \ \text{cm}^{-1}RH​=1.097×105 cm−1 is the Rydberg constant in spectroscopic units.

Hydrogen-like atoms¶

  • For one-electron atoms such as He+He^{+}He+ and Li2+Li^{2+}Li2+, the Bohr model still works, but we have to account for the increased nuclear charge ZZZ.

En=−13.6Z2n2,[eV]E_n = -13.6 \frac{Z^2}{n^2}, [eV]En​=−13.6n2Z2​,[eV]
(25)

  • E.g. for the HHH atom Z=1Z=1Z=1, for He+He^{+}He+ Z=2Z=2Z=2, etc.

Problems¶

Problem 1¶

The so-called Lyman series of lines in the emission spectrum of hydrogen corresponds to transitions from various excited states to the n = 1 orbit. Calculate the wavelength of the lowest-energy line in the Lyman series to three significant figures. In what region of the electromagnetic spectrum does it occur?

Solution

A We can use the Rydberg equation to calculate the wavelength for the Lyman series, n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1.

1λ=RH(1n12−1n22)\dfrac{1}{\lambda }=R_H \left ( \dfrac{1}{n_{1}^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right )λ1​=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)
(26)

The lowest energy results from a transition to or from the nearest energy level, hence n2=n1+1n_2 = n_1+1n2​=n1​+1.

1λ=RH(1n12−1n22)=1.097×107 m−1(11−14)=8.228×106  m−1\begin{align*} \dfrac{1}{\lambda } &=R_H \left ( \dfrac{1}{n_{1}^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right ) \\[4pt] &=1.097 \times 10^{7}\, m^{-1}\left ( \dfrac{1}{1}-\dfrac{1}{4} \right )\\[4pt] &= 8.228 \times 10^{6}\; m^{-1} \end{align*}λ1​​=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)=1.097×107m−1(11​−41​)=8.228×106m−1​
(27)

Spectroscopists often talk about energy and frequency as equivalent. The cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1 unit (wavenumbers) is particularly convenient. We can convert the answer in part A to cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1

ν~=1λ=8.228×106m−1(m100  cm)=82,280 cm−1\begin{align*} \widetilde{\nu} &=\dfrac{1}{\lambda } \\[4pt] &= 8.228\times 10^{6}\cancel{m^{-1}}\left (\dfrac{\cancel{m}}{100\;cm} \right ) \\[4pt] &= 82,280\: cm^{-1} \end{align*}ν​=λ1​=8.228×106m−1(100cmm​​)=82,280cm−1​
(28)

and

λ=1.215×10−7  m=122  nm\lambda = 1.215 \times 10^{−7}\; m = 122 \,\,nmλ=1.215×10−7m=122nm
(29)

This emission line is called Lyman alpha. It is the strongest atomic emission line from the Sun and drives the chemistry of the upper atmosphere of all the planets, producing ions by stripping electrons from atoms and molecules. It is completely absorbed by oxygen in the upper stratosphere, dissociating O2 molecules into O atoms, which react with other O2 molecules to form stratospheric ozone.

B This wavelength is in the UV region of the spectrum.

Problem 2¶

  • A. Calculate the energy of a photon that is produced when an electron in a hydrogen atom goes from an orbit with n=4n=4n=4 to an orbit with n=1n=1n=1.

  • B. What happens to the energy of the photon as the initial value of nnn approaches infinity?

Solution

A. We will use Bohr’s formula in electron volts, En=−19.61n2E_n = -19.6 \frac{1}{n^2}En​=−19.6n21​, to calculate the energy of a photon.

ΔE=19.6(112−142)=19.6⋅0.9375=18.375  ev\Delta E = 19.6 \Big ( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{4^2} \Big) = 19.6 \cdot 0.9375 = 18.375\,\, evΔE=19.6(121​−421​)=19.6⋅0.9375=18.375ev
(30)

B. The energy of the photon goes up as we excite the electron to higher and higher levels. As n2→∞n_2\rightarrow \inftyn2​→∞ we end up with a photon that has sufficient energy to ionize the atom. E=19.6⋅(112−1∞)=19.6evE = 19.6 \cdot ( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{\infty} \Big) = 19.6 evE=19.6⋅(121​−∞1​)=19.6ev

Problem 3¶

Use Rydberg’s formula to calculate the first few lines of the Lyman series (n1=1n_1=1n1​=1).

Solution

The Rydberg formula is given by:

1λ=RH(1n12−1n22)\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)λ1​=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)
(31)

For the Lyman series, n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1, and n2=2,3,4,…n_2 = 2, 3, 4, \dotsn2​=2,3,4,…. The Rydberg constant for hydrogen is:

RH=1.097×107 m−1R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \ \text{m}^{-1}RH​=1.097×107 m−1
(32)

First few lines of the Lyman series:

For n2=2n_2 = 2n2​=2:

1λ=1.097×107(112−122)\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right)λ1​=1.097×107(121​−221​)
(33)
λ=1.2157×10−7 m=121.57 nm\lambda = 1.2157 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m} = 121.57 \ \text{nm}λ=1.2157×10−7 m=121.57 nm
(34)

For n2=3n_2 = 3n2​=3:

1λ=1.097×107(112−132)\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{3^2} \right)λ1​=1.097×107(121​−321​)
(35)
λ=1.0257×10−7 m=102.57 nm\lambda = 1.0257 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m} = 102.57 \ \text{nm}λ=1.0257×10−7 m=102.57 nm
(36)

For n2=4n_2 = 4n2​=4:

1λ=1.097×107(112−142)\frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.097 \times 10^7 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{4^2} \right)λ1​=1.097×107(121​−421​)
(37)
λ=9.724×10−8 m=97.24 nm\lambda = 9.724 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{m} = 97.24 \ \text{nm}λ=9.724×10−8 m=97.24 nm
(38)

So, the first three wavelengths of the Lyman series are approximately 121.57 nm, 102.57 nm, and 97.24 nm.

Problem 4¶

A line in the Lyman series of hydrogen has a wavelength of 1.03⋅10−7m1.03 \cdot 10^{-7} m1.03⋅10−7m. Find the original level of the electron.

Solution

We are given a wavelength λ=1.03×10−7 m\lambda = 1.03 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}λ=1.03×10−7 m and asked to find the original level n2n_2n2​ of the electron in the Lyman series (where n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1).

Using the Rydberg formula:

1λ=RH(1n12−1n22)\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)λ1​=RH​(n12​1​−n22​1​)
(39)

For the Lyman series, n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1, so the equation becomes:

1λ=RH(1−1n22)\frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( 1 - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)λ1​=RH​(1−n22​1​)
(40)

Rearranging to solve for n2n_2n2​:

1n22=1−1RHλ\frac{1}{n_2^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{R_H \lambda}n22​1​=1−RH​λ1​
(41)

Substituting the values:

RH=1.097×107 m−1R_H = 1.097 \times 10^7 \ \text{m}^{-1}RH​=1.097×107 m−1
(42)
1n22=1−1(1.097×107)×(1.03×10−7)\frac{1}{n_2^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{(1.097 \times 10^7) \times (1.03 \times 10^{-7})}n22​1​=1−(1.097×107)×(1.03×10−7)1​
(43)
1n22=1−11.13091=1−0.884=0.116\frac{1}{n_2^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.13091} = 1 - 0.884 = 0.116n22​1​=1−1.130911​=1−0.884=0.116
(44)

Now, solving for n2n_2n2​:

n22=10.116=8.621n_2^2 = \frac{1}{0.116} = 8.621n22​=0.1161​=8.621
(45)
n2=8.621≈2.94n_2 = \sqrt{8.621} \approx 2.94n2​=8.621​≈2.94
(46)

Since n2n_2n2​ must be an integer, we round it to n2=3n_2 = 3n2​=3.

Thus, the original level of the electron is n2=3n_2 = 3n2​=3.

Problem 5¶

Using Bohr theory calculate ionization energy of singly ionized helium He+He^{+}He+

Solution

The ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from its ground state to infinity. Using Bohr’s theory, the energy of an electron in an orbit is given by:

En=−Z2RHn2E_n = -\frac{Z^2 R_H}{n^2}En​=−n2Z2RH​​
(47)

Where:

  • ZZZ is the atomic number,

  • RH=13.6 eVR_H = 13.6 \ \text{eV}RH​=13.6 eV is the Rydberg constant for hydrogen,

  • nnn is the principal quantum number.

For singly ionized helium He+He^+He+, the atomic number Z=2Z = 2Z=2. In the ground state, n=1n = 1n=1.

Thus, the energy in the ground state is:

E1=−Z2RH12=−(2)2×13.6 eV12=−4×13.6 eV=−54.4 eVE_1 = -\frac{Z^2 R_H}{1^2} = -\frac{(2)^2 \times 13.6 \ \text{eV}}{1^2} = -4 \times 13.6 \ \text{eV} = -54.4 \ \text{eV}E1​=−12Z2RH​​=−12(2)2×13.6 eV​=−4×13.6 eV=−54.4 eV
(48)

The ionization energy is the negative of this ground state energy (since we want to bring the electron to n=∞n = \inftyn=∞):

Eionization=54.4 eVE_{\text{ionization}} = 54.4 \ \text{eV}Eionization​=54.4 eV
(49)

Therefore, the ionization energy of singly ionized helium He+He^+He+ is 54.4 eV54.4 \ \text{eV}54.4 eV.

Problem 6¶

  • Calculate radii of Bohr orbit for first few levels.

  • (Optional) Using python plot rnr_nrn​ vs nnn

Solution

The radius of the Bohr orbit is given by the formula:

rn=n2a0Zr_n = \frac{n^2 a_0}{Z}rn​=Zn2a0​​
(50)

Where:

  • nnn is the principal quantum number (level),

  • a0=5.29×10−11 ma_0 = 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}a0​=5.29×10−11 m is the Bohr radius for hydrogen,

  • ZZZ is the atomic number (for hydrogen, Z=1Z = 1Z=1).

For hydrogen (Z=1Z = 1Z=1), the radii for the first few levels are:

For n=1n = 1n=1:

r1=12×5.29×10−11 m1=5.29×10−11 mr_1 = \frac{1^2 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}}{1} = 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}r1​=112×5.29×10−11 m​=5.29×10−11 m
(51)

For n=2n = 2n=2:

r2=22×5.29×10−11 m1=4×5.29×10−11 m=2.116×10−10 mr_2 = \frac{2^2 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}}{1} = 4 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m} = 2.116 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}r2​=122×5.29×10−11 m​=4×5.29×10−11 m=2.116×10−10 m
(52)

For n=3n = 3n=3:

r3=32×5.29×10−11 m1=9×5.29×10−11 m=4.761×10−10 mr_3 = \frac{3^2 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}}{1} = 9 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m} = 4.761 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}r3​=132×5.29×10−11 m​=9×5.29×10−11 m=4.761×10−10 m
(53)

For n=4n = 4n=4:

r4=42×5.29×10−11 m1=16×5.29×10−11 m=8.464×10−10 mr_4 = \frac{4^2 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}}{1} = 16 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m} = 8.464 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}r4​=142×5.29×10−11 m​=16×5.29×10−11 m=8.464×10−10 m
(54)

Therefore, the radii of the Bohr orbits for the first few levels are:

  • r1=5.29×10−11 mr_1 = 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}r1​=5.29×10−11 m,

  • r2=2.116×10−10 mr_2 = 2.116 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}r2​=2.116×10−10 m,

  • r3=4.761×10−10 mr_3 = 4.761 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}r3​=4.761×10−10 m,
